title: Macramé door curtain panel
subtitle: A full-length knotted panel to hang in a doorway or window
excerpt: Work alternating square knots in pairs of 5 mm cotton cord to make a full-length panel that hangs as a decorative room divider or door screen. The finished piece is about 90 cm wide and 200 cm long, giving privacy without blocking light.
sourceNotes: Macramé door curtain and room-divider panels synthesised from contemporary British macramé practice. Large knotted panels are a traditional application of macramé technique documented in 1970s pattern publications.

glossaryTerms.definition: The core macramé knot: two working cords tied around two filler cords, first left over right, then right over left. Alternating square knots across a wide warp produces the standard open-mesh macramé fabric.
A mounting knot for attaching folded cords to a dowel or rod. The folded cord's loop passes over the rod and the two tails are pulled through the loop and down.
The cords that run the full length of a macramé panel, mounted at the top and hanging down. Working cords tie around the warp to build the knotted structure.

content.text: A door curtain panel uses the same alternating
structure as a wall hanging, simply scaled up in width and length. The
are mounted on a wooden or metal rod using
. Calculate the number of cords needed at 4 cords (2 pairs) per 3 cm of finished width for a 5 mm cord.

items.name: Wooden dowel or metal rod, 100 cm long
Two cup hooks or wall brackets for mounting

content.text: Step 1: Calculate and cut the cords

For a 90 cm wide, 200 cm long panel in alternating square knots, cut 120 cords each 5 m long. Mount all cords onto the dowel with lark's head knots, spaced evenly to fill the 90 cm width. This gives 240 working ends.

Work in alternating square-knot rows with a 3 cm gap between rows. Row 1: group cords in sets of 4 from left to right and tie one square knot per group across the full width. Row 2: skip the first 2 cords, group the remaining cords in sets of 4, and tie one square knot per group. This offset creates the characteristic diamond mesh pattern. Continue alternating rows for the full 200 cm length.
Trim the cord ends to an even length below the last knot row. For a straight hem, cut all cords level. For a V-shape or diagonal, use a ruler and chalk line to mark the cutting line before trimming.

items.problem: The panel narrows toward the bottom
items.cause: Cord length ran out before the full length was reached, or the knots were tied too tightly, pulling the panel inward
items.fix: Keep knot tension consistent and moderate throughout. Check the width every 50 cm. If the width decreases, loosen the most recent rows by gently pulling the working cords before the knots set fully.

items.problem: The panel is too stiff and does not hang with a good drape
items.cause: The cord is too thick, the knot spacing too close, or the tension too tight
items.fix: For panels intended to hang and drape, a 4 mm cord with 4 cm row spacing is softer than 5 mm at 3 cm spacing. Increasing row spacing also speeds up the work considerably on a large piece.
